does: This paper introduces realism win rate as a quantitative metric for measuring how distinguishable auditor transcripts are from actual deployment interactions, and empirically evaluates whether providing auditors with real deployment resources improves the ecological validity and effectiveness of code auditing. The work directly measures realism in audit scenarios by comparing transcripts generated under different resource conditions, establishing a foundation for understanding what factors make audits more representative of genuine production settings.
connects: This research builds on prior work in software auditing and quality assurance that has grappled with the gap between controlled testing environments and real-world deployment conditions. It extends existing audit evaluation frameworks by introducing a concrete metric to quantify realism—a dimension previously difficult to operationalize. The paper responds to longstanding concerns about audit validity by investigating whether realistic deployment resources meaningfully affect how auditors interact with code systems, drawing on broader literature examining simulation fidelity and ecological validity in security and quality assurance practices.
enables: This work enables more rigorous evaluation of code audit methodologies by providing a quantifiable measure of realism that future research can optimize for. It creates a foundation for developing deployment-resource-aware audit frameworks that better approximate production conditions while remaining tractable to execute. The realism metric could inform the design of audit tools and protocols that incorporate relevant deployment constraints, potentially improving the generalizability of audit findings to actual systems and paving the way for more reliable security and quality assessments in real software engineering contexts.
